EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist front dropped a borderline warning level amount of snow at elevations higher then 2000 ft along the Klondike Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Moore Creek Bridge SNOTEL at 2250 ft along the Klondike Highway measured 6 inches of snow by 9am on the 9th and an additional 2 inches through the early afternoon. DOT reported similar numbers near the Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved into the southern Panhandle from the SW on the 28th bringing in some moderate to heavy precipitation. The Hyder area near the southern border with Canada kept northerly flow and temperatures cold enough for snow until the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An RWIS station just over 3 miles from the border with Hyder recorded around 12.5 inches of snow between 11am on the 28th and 2am on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow showers tracked over the southern panhandle early Friday morning with quickly accumulating snow. The snow showers fell in two bands, one across the southern panhandle and another across Sitka to Juneau on Friday. The northern band very slowly track northward to Yakutat late Friday night into early Saturday morning causing an advisory for Yakutat to be upgraded to a warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow quickly became moderate to heavy with several inches stacking up between 6 to 9am. NWS Annette Island measured 6.7 inches had fallen at the Ferry Terminal by 848am on the 3rd. Amounts were lower in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow showers tracked over the southern panhandle early Friday morning with quickly accumulating snow. The snow showers fell in two bands, one across the southern panhandle and another across Sitka to Juneau on Friday. The northern band very slowly track northward to Yakutat late Friday night into early Saturday morning causing an advisory for Yakutat to be upgraded to a warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers fell over Yakutat the evening of the 3rd through the early morning with reports ranging from 5 to 6.4 inches across town. Snow began again in the late morning but accumulation rates were lower with only an additional 1 to 2.4 inches falling by the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a two week period of cold and dry weather, a front settled over the NE Gulf Coast bringing dry fluffy snow that stacked up in Yakutat through the morning of Valentine's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late in the night on the 13th and became heavy through the early morning hours of the 14th, reaching 10 inches by 830am. Heavy snow continued through the mid-day with winds gusting to around 25mph and visibilities down to a half mile. By the time snow ended in the late afternoon, the total accumulation was 14.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high end gale to storm force system moved in from the south, bringing damaging winds in the morning of the 26th to the southern panhandle while heavy precipitation rates brought snow to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain began across Skagway around dawn on the 26th and as rates picked up changed to snow. At the upper elevations of the Klondike Highway, DOT reported up to 5 inches by 426pm with very difficult driving conditions. The following morning DOT report showed 6 to 8 inches of snow between mile post 12 and the border. Similarly, the area avalanche report noted around 8 inches of snow had fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska brough winds, heavy precipitation rates, and rising snow levels. This lead to heavy snow for the White Pass area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to an areawide internet outage, timing of snowfall is estimated based on automated snow depth sensors. The morning Klondike Highway report from DOT at 807am on the 28th noted difficult travel conditions and a range of 1 to 8 inches of snow between 12 mile and the border, and up to 6 inches between mile markers 8 to 12. This range of speeds may imply drifting snow and snow continued until later that afternoon. Another observation was not available, and total accumulation is unknown. Before observations dropped off at around 9pm on the 27th, the White Pass RWIS was gusting to 37mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March began with a strong low pressure system moving into the southeastern Bearing Sea. This storm system stalled along Alaska's west coast for several days as a large high pressure system remained stationary across the Beaufort Sea. Steady snow fell along the west coast for several days while an increasing pressure gradient resulted in very strong east and northeast winds that brough blizzard conditions to may communities along the west coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PAVA reported winds greater than35 mph and visibilities of 1/4 mile or less from 4:40 PM to 8:20 PM on March 2nd. During the height of the blizzard, winds were sustained at 47 mph with gusts to 53 mph and visibility below 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved onto the Bearing Coastline during the morning hours of March 30th. Steady snowfall quickly changed over to mixed precipitation with freezing rain becoming the primary precipitation type through the day. Ice accumulated on cars and power lines as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nome (POAM) metar at 9:53 am reported 0.13 of ice. The Alaska State Troopers in Nome confirmed that there was a glaze of ice on cars, rails, and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March began with a strong low pressure system moving into the southeastern Bearing Sea. This storm system stalled along Alaska's west coast for several days as a large high pressure system remained stationary across the Beaufort Sea. Steady snow fell along the west coast for several days while an increasing pressure gradient resulted in very strong east and northeast winds that brough blizzard conditions to may communities along the west coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at PFKT (Brevig Mission Airport) prior to dawn and continued through midday. The visibility remained at or below 1/4 mile with falling snow and winds gusting as high as 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March began with a strong low pressure system moving into the southeastern Bearing Sea. This storm system stalled along Alaska's west coast for several days as a large high pressure system remained stationary across the Beaufort Sea. Steady snow fell along the west coast for several days while an increasing pressure gradient resulted in very strong east and northeast winds that brough blizzard conditions to may communities along the west coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kivalina AWOS (PAVL) reported winds in excess of 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph throughout the day. Heavy snow from the previous few days began to blow and the visibility remained at or below 1/4 mile for more than 8 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three strong low pressure centers moved northward through the Bearing with the last one pushing a strong cold front into the interior. These storms resulted in heavy snowfall across western and northern Alaska with a mix of snow a freezing rain throughout the interior. Strong winds also occurred creating blizzard conditions across several communities over the course of five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska DOT reported on the 511 site that numerous avalanches had occurred through Atigun pass resulting in closure of the Dalton Highway. During the time of the event the Chandalar Shelf mesonet site was reporting several hours of 30 to 40 mph wind gusts leading to extra snow loading in the area.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS AND HIGH TERRAIN SOUTH OF THE YUKON RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three strong low pressure centers moved northward through the Bearing with the last one pushing a strong cold front into the interior. These storms resulted in heavy snowfall across western and northern Alaska with a mix of snow a freezing rain throughout the interior. Strong winds also occurred creating blizzard conditions across several communities over the course of five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Nome Creek SNOWTEL reported warning level snowfall on both the 24th and 25th. Over the course of the event 33 inches of snow was recorded. Alaska DOT closed the Elliott Highway at Eagle Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three strong low pressure centers moved northward through the Bearing with the last one pushing a strong cold front into the interior. These storms resulted in heavy snowfall across western and northern Alaska with a mix of snow a freezing rain throughout the interior. Strong winds also occurred creating blizzard conditions across several communities over the course of five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 71 mph at Ft Greely (PABI) with other locations consistently experiencing wind gusts in excess of 50 mph for several hours. Numerous trees were downed as well as multiple power outages in and around Delta Junction. As many as 900 residents were without power as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From January 23rd to January 26th, a cluster of low pressure systems with multiple separate centers moved into the Bering Sea. Parts of this cluster included a low with two centers that moved in from the west and a north Pacific Low that rose from the Southwest. As they approached the Southwestern Alaskan mainland, a frontal system set up and pressure gradients oriented in such a way that winds became gusty in the Alaska Peninsula during the afternoon hours of January 23rd. The lows combined and took a northerly turn on the 24th. The frontal system remained in place for the next two days and another north Pacific low rose from the south on January 25th. The low stalled out over the Alaska Peninsula and took advantage of an atmospheric river to drive heavy snowfall into Southwest and Southcentral Alaska through January 26th. A period of gusty winds were also felt in the Anchorage region on January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska Department of Transportation closed Thompson Pass from Milepost 12 to Milepost 46 at 8:00 AM AKST January 26th due to avalanche danger and inclement weather. The Department of Transportation 511 website reported Very Difficult road conditions the same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From January 23rd to January 26th, a cluster of low pressure systems with multiple separate centers moved into the Bering Sea. Parts of this cluster included a low with two centers that moved in from the west and a north Pacific Low that rose from the Southwest. As they approached the Southwestern Alaskan mainland, a frontal system set up and pressure gradients oriented in such a way that winds became gusty in the Alaska Peninsula during the afternoon hours of January 23rd. A period of gusty winds were also felt in the Anchorage region on January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk ASOS Station (PAMY) reported heavy snow, gusty winds over 40 mph and visibility below one quarter of a mile from 10:44 AM AKST to past 2:30 PM AKST on January 26th. Webcams in the village confirmed this low visibility. Nunapitchuk Airport (PPIT) similarly reported blizzard conditions with winds gusting to 45 to 50 mph and quarter mile visibility from 9:20 PM AKST January 26 to 12:00 AM AKST January 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and strong low moved up the North Pacific into the eastern Bering Sea, peaking in strength as it moved up the Kuskokwim Delta. The orientation of the low's front was ideal for strong winds across Southcentral Alaska. Damaging winds were observed across parts of the Turnagain Arm, Kenai Peninsula, Matanuska Valley, and Anchorage. A significant amount of moisture also was transported by this system, causing issues with localized flooding and ponding of water on roads across parts of Southcentral. Snow fell in higher elevations, with multiple feet recorded in Thompson Pass. Colder temperatures prevailed after the front passed, allowing for heavy snowfall in Whittier on the morning of January 12. In Southwest Alaska, high surf, and minor coastal flooding in the Kuskokwim Delta Coast resulted from strong onshore winds. Low sea ice coverage was a factor in this flooding episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Portage ASOS Station (PATO) reported strong winds that frequently gusted to over 70 mph for a four hour period between 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM AKST on January 12th. Other stations around the same area reported similar wind speeds in the same timeframe. Alyeska Resort had even higher winds with  gusts over 100 mph from 4:00 AM to 9:00 AM AKST the same day. An article from Alaska Public Media reported that Alyeska Resort closed, citing extreme winds, downed trees, and debris on the slopes which make conditions unsafe for skiing and snowboarding. Link to the Alaska Public article: https://alaskapublic.org/news/anchorage/2025-01-13/you-cant-sit-and-relax-anchorage-cleans-up-after-turbulent-winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low's front produced a strong barrier jet that brought gusty winds of over 70 mph in the Cordova airport and Cordova itself. A weather station located at the northern periphery of Cordova reported strong winds gusting to over 65 mph for a two hour period from 8:00 AM to 9:00 AM AKST on January 12th. The highest wind gust was 74 mph at 8:12 AM AKST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north Pacific low moved into the Bering and tracked northeastward into the Kuskokwim Delta Coast. Southerly winds increased to gale force strength and combined with high tides and a lack of sea ice to cause coastal flooding that inundated many boardwalks and low lying areas in Kwigillingok, Alaska on January 10th. Snow machine trails were submerged as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kwigillingok, Alaska saw major flooding on January 10th. A tribal resilience coordinator reported that water levels were the highest ... seen thus far. He continued: A group of Russian Orthodox Christians, observing Slaviq (a Russian Orthodox equivalent to Christmas), were forced to stay put in a house because there were numerous spots too deep to cross on boardwalks. Snow machine trails were all water as well. A search and rescue worker reported 4 - 8 ft of water. An article from Alaska Public Media reported that the Native Village of Kwigillingok Tribal Administrator noted that multiple boardwalks were submerged in the lower part of the community. Alaska Public Media article link: https://alaskapublic.org/2025-01-13/tidal-action-and-fierce-winds-flood-low-lying-areas-of-kwigillingok.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below average sea ice extent, including shorefast ice, was insufficient to properly shelter communities along the Kuskokwim Delta coast from storm surge. A Kwigillingok community coordinator shared pictures showcasing flooding that occurred on January 12th. Flooding continued into January 13th, coinciding with the high tide. The community leader noted that water levels were worse than a recent flooding event that took place on January 10th.

The Department of Transportation reported 108 inches of snow over a three day period from January 10th to January 13th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of downed spruce trees on Pittman Rd and Church Rd. The Glenn Highway weather station near the Matanuska Lake reported strong winds with a peak wind gust of 73 mph at 3:20 AM AKST. Social media posts reported downed trees and roof damage. There were many LSRs issued for damage to small outbuildings and trees for areas near Palmer, Wasilla, and Butte. An article from Alaska Public Media reported that four Mat-Su schools were closed, with most schools having a delayed start on January 16th. Link to Alaska Public Media article: https://alaskapublic.org/news/anchorage/2025-01-13/anchorage-schools-closed-after-day-of-damaging-winds-rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of very warm weather mobilized ice on the Anchor River, which caused rapid melting in January that loosened ice on the river and caused numerous ice jams along the river as ice moved from the upstream Anchor River gauge to the downstream gauge. This ice eventually flushed out by the end of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm weather caused the breakup of ice on the Anchor River near Anchor Point.  Initially river rises were reported on the portion of the Anchor River between the Sterling Highway Bridge and the Old Sterling Highway bridge, as ice moved downstream.  Ice eventually became entrenched below the old Sterling Highway bridge, flooding the Anchor Point road and compromising residents ability to reach their homes.  Additional widespread flooding was reported in the  Anchor River Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche slid into the Little Susitna River blocking much of the river flow and flooding roads and a campground upstream along the riverbank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche slid into the Little Susitna River blocking the river flow and flooding roads near the river; including the Government Peak Campground.  River flow would eventually erode the blockage and restore flow to the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Anchor River Basin caused additional rises on the Anchor River, from areas just north of Homer to Anchor Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Anchor River basin brought river levels up quickly and flooded many  areas along the river between Anchor Point and Homer.  The Blackwater Bend area reported water over the Sterling Highway. Additional severe flooding was reported on Anchor Point Road near the Old Sterling Highway as water reactivated ice in the Anchor River, causing an ice jam.  Over a foot of water spread over Anchor Point Road, which closed access to homes along the road.  The entire Anchor River Campground was flooded as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions occurred in Whittier (WMHA2) between 9:00 AM and 12:00 PM AKST on January 12th. Winds gusted to over 60 mph with visibility dropping to a quarter of a mile during this timeframe until the weather sensor failed. There were also reports of heavy snow and winds along portions of the Seward Highway the same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kuskokwim Delta coast and the western capes of Bristol Bay were affected by a shortwave trough moving in from the Bering Sea ahead of a ridge. Gusty gale force winds and heavy snowfall resulted as the trough moved inland. These factors led to blizzard conditions as well as very cold wind chills that impacted the aforementioned regions. Residual cold air from a prior Arctic low was another factor in the formation of these weather events. Whiteout conditions affected various communities across the coastal Kuskokwim Delta and the western capes of Bristol Bay for several hours on January 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk ASOS station (PAMY) reported 0 to quarter mile visibilities and winds gusting to the mid 40s for several hours from 7:30 AM AKST to 2:30 PM AKST on January 30th. Multiple webcams along the coast from Newtok to Goodnews Bay revealed whiteout conditions in the morning hours of January 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic low passed through Southcentral from the north from January 28th to January 30th. Snow chances and wind speeds increased as a result, particularly in the Copper River Basin from Paxson to Isabel Pass and Prince William Sound including Thompson Pass. Broad Pass had lower chances for snowfall, but a previous snow event meant that easily transportable snowfall was present in the pass. All three passes ended up in blizzard conditions between the 29th and 30th of January, creating very difficult driving conditions from blowing snow and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee of the Alaska Department of Transportation called the National Weather Service, reporting sustained quarter mile visibility and blizzard conditions in Thompson Pass. Additionally, the 511 site of the Department of Transportation reported Very Difficult driving conditions for Thompson Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska Department of Transportation 511 site reported Very Difficult driving conditions near Paxson and along Isabel Pass due to reduced visibility, strong winds, blowing snow, and drifting snow at 3:00 AM AKST January 29th. Isabel Pass was closed by the Department of Transportation on January 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems impacted Southcentral from January 6th to January 7th. Southerly flow from a surface low brought warmer air into the region, increasing temperatures, particularly aloft, which made conditions favorable for mixed precipitation including freezing rain. A trough with its associated front pushed into the Southcentral coast the morning of the 6th, bringing heavy precipitation and even more warm air to Southcentral. Locations including Valdez, Thompson Pass, Anchorage, the Matanuska Valley and West Kenai Peninsula saw mixed precipitation including freezing rain that led to difficult driving conditions and accidents. This was followed by a strong low that pushed into the Gulf in the afternoon hours of the 6th, which brought hurricane force winds to Southcentral. The Anchorage Hillside, Portage, and East Turnagain Arm areas received very strong winds that gusted in excess of 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Department of Transportation employee reported rain with some snow mixed in a the top of Thompson Pass. Another employee of the Department of Transportation reported icy roads along the Richardson Highway. He also reported vehicles in ditches along the highway. A Spotter from Valdez reported freezing rain in Thompson Pass on the morning of January 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Anchorage ASOS Station (PANC) reported freezing rain at 12:07 PM AKST 1/6/25. PAMR and PAED similarly reported freezing rain near the same time period. Posts from social media reported graupel, freezing rain, rain, and snow. A trained spotter reported 0.1 inches of snowfall on the Anchorage Hillside. Ekluna Lake Rd closed at 12:40 PM AKST January 6th due to an extreme ice buildup and unsafe road conditions. An employee from the River Forecast Center shared a report that said The road is an unholy mess and cars are piling up at mile 4 and 4.5. Eklutna Lake Road is currently blocked by stranded cars - people can't get up or down. There were numerous social media reports of accidents and cars sliding off roads in Anchorage and Eagle River. An Anchorage police spokesperson said that 79 accidents and 54 vehicles in distress had been reported Monday January 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station in the Anchorage Hillside reported an extended period of gusty winds of over 50 mph that started January 24th and lasted until January 26th. The station reported a max wind gust for three consecutive observations from 3:11 PM AKST to 3:21 PM AKST on January 25th. Other weather stations in the area reported similarly high gusts of over 70 mph around the same timeframe. Wind gusts over the Anchorage International Airport exceeded 60 mph on January 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From January 23rd to January 26th, a cluster of low pressure systems with multiple separate centers moved into the Bering Sea. Parts of this cluster included a low with two centers that moved in from the west and a north Pacific Low that rose from the Southwest. As they approached the Southwestern Alaskan mainland, a frontal system set up and pressure gradients oriented in such a way that winds became gusty in the Alaska Peninsula during the afternoon hours of January 23rd. The lows combined and took a northerly turn on the 24th. The frontal system remained in place for the next two days and another north Pacific low rose from the south on January 25th. The low stalled out over the Alaska Peninsula and took advantage of an atmospheric river to drive heavy snowfall into Southwest and Southcentral Alaska through January 26th. A period of gusty winds were also felt in the Anchorage region on January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager from Unalaska Public safety reported that 1 to 2 feet of snowfall fell in a 24 hour period from January 25th to January 26th. He reported that snow drifts of over two and a half feet were forming. He mentioned that vehicles were getting stuck on roads and road crews were having difficulty keeping up with the snow. Visibilities were also low during this time.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS AND HIGH TERRAIN SOUTH OF THE YUKON RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the Bearing Sea into the central Yukon over the course of several days. Initially this system brought strong onshore winds to the Bearing coastline along with a mixed bag of wintry precipitation for the Seward Peninsula. As the week progressed a strong arctic high pushed onto the Arctic coastline from the north resulting in very strong easterly flow for the North Slope along with lots of blowing snow to produce widespread blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska DOT reported wind speeds of 43 mph and near zero visibility Over Eagle Summit. As a result the highway at Eagle Summit was closed for several hours until conditions improved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska and strong high pressure over the Arctic Ocean allowed for several days of strong winds along the Northwest Arctic Coast of Alaska. Off and on blizzard conditions persisted for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow combined with persistent strong northeast winds along the Northwest Arctic Coast to produce a short period of blizzard conditions at Point Lay (PPIZ).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant weather pattern with a trough over the Bering Sea and a ridge over eastern Alaska resulted in a continuation of gusty winds across the northwest Arctic coastline. At times these winds combined with snow showers to produce additional blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds combined with light snow during the early afternoon hours to reduce the visibility to 1/4 mile, resulting in blizzard conditions at Point Lay (PPIZ). These conditions persisted for the rest of the day.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN NORTON SOUND AND NULATO HILLS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system dropped southwestwards across northern and central Alaska which brought a potent cold front through the Norton Sound. Northeast offshore winds over the eastern Norton Sound combined with light snow to produce blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north and northwest winds combined with periods of moderate snowfall to create an extended period of blizzard conditions. Wind gusts in excess of 35 mph and visibilities around 1/4 mile persisted for for almost 30 hours at Shaktoolik (PFSH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather systems impacted Southcentral from January 6th to January 7th. Southerly flow from a surface low brought warmer air into the region, increasing temperatures, particularly aloft, which made conditions favorable for mixed precipitation including freezing rain. A trough with its associated front pushed into the Southcentral coast the morning of the 6th, bringing heavy precipitation and even more warm air to Southcentral. Locations including Valdez, Thompson Pass, Anchorage, the Matanuska Valley and West Kenai Peninsula saw mixed precipitation including freezing rain that led to difficult driving conditions and accidents. This was followed by a strong low that pushed into the Gulf in the afternoon hours of the 6th, which brought hurricane force winds to Southcentral. The Anchorage Hillside, Portage, and East Turnagain Arm areas received very strong winds that gusted in excess of 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from social media revealed areas of ice on roadways. Social media also reported that a school bus slid off the road in Butte. The Mat-Su Borough Planning Department had to cancel its January 6th meeting due to inclement weather. The Palmer-based Alaska State Troopers responded to more than two dozen collisions between 6 AM and 6 PM AKST on Monday, January 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Talkeetna ASOS Station (PATK) reported light rain at 34F at 11:10 PM AKST. A CoCoRAHS observer/spotter along Oil Well Road southwest of Trapper Creek reported overflow and standing water on trails that made travel hazardous in the backcountry. Another CoCoRAHS observer near Talkeetna reported a mix of rain, sleet, and freezing rain that began on the morning of January 7th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee in Nome reported steady freezing rain through the morning hours with .25 inch accumulation of ice at the noon observation. Roads were reported to be very slick with heavy ice accumulations on trees and power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds combined with heavy falling snow to produce blizzard conditions through the afternoon and into the night. Visibilities remained at or below 1/4 mile for an extended time period with wind gusts consistently at or above 40 mph at Nuiqsut (PAQT. Meanwhile at Deadhorse (PASC) winds gusted as high as 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station (PMHA2) on the Anchorage Hillside reported a peak wind gust of 82 mph at 12:52 AM AKST January 7th. More gusts of over 70 mph were reported by this same station for the next 30 minutes afterwards. Other stations nearby reported wind gusts exceeding 70 mph near the same time period. Winds peaked just after midnight as the low moved inland and pressure began to rise.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Portage ASOS (PATO) reported a wind gust measuring 82 mph at 12:31 PM AKST January 6th. Weather stations at Alyeska Resort reported wind gusts of over 90 mph around the same time. High winds led to the closure of Alyeska Resort due to a power outage. An animal shelter at the Alaska Wildlife Conservation Center was destroyed by high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cordova ASOS (PACV) reported wind gusts of over 70 mph at from 3:50 PM to 4:53 PM AKST on January 6th as the front moved onshore. A peak wind gust of 82 mph was reported by the station at 4:53 PM AKST. A weather station (CRVA2) within Cordova itself reported very strong wind gusts of over 80 mph with a peak wind gust of 88 mph around the same time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of surface low pressure systems tracking north into Alaska's interior continued to bring periods of heavy snow and strong winds to the northern interior and along portions of the Dalton Highway through the central Brooks Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall continued through the morning hours with wind speeds increasing into the afternoon to create very low visibilities at Atigun Pass. A local observer reported near white out conditions and visibilities well below 1/4 miles several hours. The local Department of Transportation reported very difficult driving conditions along the Dalton Highway through the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low over the eastern Aleutians in tandem with a strong Siberian high produced string south winds through the interior and Alaska Range while driving a potent front across the Bering and into portions of western Alaska producing locally heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds blew through Windy Pass reaching a top speed of 72 mph at ACRS2 (Antler Creek) observation tower.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN ALASKA RANGE NORTH OF TRIMS CAMP
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low over the eastern Aleutians in tandem with a strong Siberian high produced string south winds through the interior and Alaska Range while driving a potent front across the Bering and into portions of western Alaska producing locally heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gap winds developed through Isabel Pass during the overnight hours with wind speeds near Fort Greely at site OPFA2 peaking at 86 mph during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low over the eastern Aleutians in tandem with a strong Siberian high produced string south winds through the interior and Alaska Range while driving a potent front across the Bering and into portions of western Alaska producing locally heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTEL data from the Seward Peninsula at Paragon Creek reported total snowfall of 8 with additional light snow still falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low over the eastern Aleutians in tandem with a strong Siberian high produced string south winds through the interior and Alaska Range while driving a potent front across the Bering and into portions of western Alaska producing locally heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds through Isabel Pass emerged into the Delta Junction area with a station MGTA2 at the Fort Greely main gate seeing about an hour of wind gusts near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold upper level low tracked from the Bering Coastline of Western Alaska into the interior towards the end of the month. This resulted in periods of locally heavy snow and strong winds which brought low visibilities due to blowing snow. In addition, the coldest air of the season arrived across northern Alaska in the wake of this storm as a large Arctic high settled in behind the storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chevak Airport AWOS (PAVA) reported Winter Storm conditions with visibilities dropping to between 1/4 and 1/2 mile for more than 12 hours while reporting heavy snow and fog. Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph were common during this time frame.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH SLOPES OF THE CENTRAL BROOKS RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three strong low pressure centers moved northward through the Bearing with the last one pushing a strong cold front into the interior. These storms resulted in heavy snowfall across western and northern Alaska with a mix of snow a freezing rain throughout the interior. Strong winds also occurred creating blizzard conditions across several communities over the course of five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wiseman COOP observer reported 13.2 inches of new snow for the 24 hours ending at 8am on January 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three strong low pressure centers moved northward through the Bearing with the last one pushing a strong cold front into the interior. Strong winds also occurred creating blizzard conditions across several communities over the course of five days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bettles Field SNOTEL reported 8 inches of snow in 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atqasuk AWOW PATQ reported several hours of blizzard conditions with visibility as low as .13 mile and wind gusts as high as 55 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska DOT reported on their 511 site that an Avalanche occurred at mile 219 of the Parks Highway and was blocking traffic in all directions. DOT estimated that at least 6 feet of snow was covering the highway as a result of the avalanche.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Minchumina reported an accumulation of 1 inch of freezing rain followed by 6 inches of snowfall on top of the ice in a 24 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow changed to freezing rain at McGrath and continued for over 30 hours. There was .94 inches of freezing rain that fell in one 24 hour period with over an inch of freezing rain total during the event. the Innoko Lodge lost power and reported ice covered trees and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and strong low moved up the North Pacific into the eastern Bering Sea, peaking in strength as it moved up the Kuskokwim Delta. The orientation of the low's front was ideal for strong winds across Southcentral Alaska. Damaging winds were observed across parts of the Turnagain Arm, Kenai Peninsula, Matanuska Valley, and Anchorage. A significant amount of moisture also was transported by this system, causing issues with localized flooding and ponding of water on roads across parts of Southcentral. Snow fell in higher elevations, with multiple feet recorded in Thompson Pass. Colder temperatures prevailed after the front passed, allowing for heavy snowfall in Whittier on the morning of January 12. In Southwest Alaska, high surf, and minor coastal flooding in the Kuskokwim Delta Coast resulted from strong onshore winds. Low sea ice coverage was a factor in this flooding episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Potter Valley and Arctic Valley weather stations (PMHA2 and ARCA2) reported wind gusts of over 80 mph for an extended period of time during the morning hours of January 12th. Arctic Valley was especially gusty and experience a wind gust of 107 mph at 11:00 AM AKST before the station stopped reported data. Other weather stations around these areas similarly reported strong wind gusts over 80 mph around the same time period. An NWS employee reported a 73 mph wind gusts at the site GW5352 ANCHORAGE, which resulted in a door to his shed being blown down. An article from Alaska Public Media reported many impacts across Anchorage including structure damage, downed powerlines which led to power outages, and down trees. One notable impact was the collapse of a pedestrian bridge in Rabbit Creek. There were two reports of injuries: a firefighter who was aiding a family in stabilizing a structure and a person who was impacted by an ambulance that was blown against them. The Anchorage School District closed schools on January 16th, citing issues related to power and slick parking lots. Link to the Alaska Public article: https://alaskapublic.org/news/anchorage/2025-01-13/you-cant-sit-and-relax-anchorage-cleans-up-after-turbulent-winds.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN ALASKA RANGE SOUTH OF TRIMS CAMP
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold upper level low tracked from the Bering Coastline of Western Alaska into the interior towards the end of the month. This resulted in periods of locally heavy snow and strong winds which brought low visibilities due to blowing snow. In addition, the coldest air of the season arrived across northern Alaska in the wake of this storm as a large Arctic high settled in behind the storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska DOT closed the Richardson Highway from mile 186 to mile 218 due to very heavy snow and extremely low visibility in blowing snow. Snowfall totaled 6 inches and winds were estimated to have been between 60 and 70 mph.

This resulted in periods of locally heavy snow and strong winds which brought low visibilities due to blowing snow. In addition, the coldest air of the season arrived across northern Alaska in the wake of this storm as a large Arctic high settled in behind the storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Savoonga AWOS (PASA) reported almost 10 hours of heavy snow and visibilities between 1/4 and 1/2 mile. Winds were sustained at 30 to 35 mph with gusts to 40 mph. On occasion visibilities dropped to below a quarter mile due to the blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold upper level low tracked from the Bering Coastline of Western Alaska into the interior towards the end of the month. This resulted in periods of locally heavy snow and strong winds which brought low visibilities due to blowing snow. In addition, the coldest air of the season arrived across northern Alaska in the wake of this storm as a large Arctic high settled in behind the storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Selawik AWOS (PASK) reported a surface temperature of -36F and 18kt wind speed resulting in a wind chill of -70F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the night of January 14th, a complex low centered near Shelikof Strait pushed a frontal system up across the Kenai Peninsula. A moderate band of snowfall resulted, which stalled over the southwestern portion of the peninsula for several hours. Homer proper saw 0.49 in of precipitation that mostly fell as snow, but accumulations were low due to warmer temperatures (32 to 34F). However, the Homer Bluffs, which are higher in elevation, had colder air and thus, higher snow ratios. Over a foot of snow fell across the Homer Bluffs between 11 PM AKST Jan 14th and 7 AM AKST Jan 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report from IRIS measured 15 inches of snow at Fritz Creek on the Homer Bluffs. Additionally, 12 inches of snow was reported at Diamond Ridge on the Bluffs via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded front moved from west to east across the Southern Bering and Aleutians/Alaska Peninsula on January 22nd. High instability was present in the atmosphere with a strong temperature inversion at middle levels. Gusty winds South/Southwesterly winds of over 70 mph were felt in Akutan Island in the afternoon hours of January 22nd. Winds peaked in Unalaska in from 8:00 AM AKST to 3:00 PM AKST January 23rd, gusting to over 70 mph during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A C-MAN Station in Akutan reported a wind gust of 82 mph at 4:42 PM AKST on January 22nd. Winds were gusting to over 70 mph for two hours before this peak wind gust was reported and continued to gust to over 70 mph until 6:00 PM AKST the same day. Another C-MAN station in Unalaska reported gusty winds of over 70 mph starting at 8:42 AM AKST January 23rd; lasting until 3:00 PM AKST the same day. The peak wind for this station was 78 mph at 11:22 AM AKST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kuskokwim Delta coast and the western capes of Bristol Bay were affected by a shortwave trough moving in from the Bering Sea ahead of a ridge. Gusty gale force winds and heavy snowfall resulted as the trough moved inland. These factors led to blizzard conditions as well as very cold wind chills that impacted the aforementioned regions. Residual cold air from a prior Arctic low was another factor in the formation of these weather events. Whiteout conditions affected various communities across the coastal Kuskokwim Delta and the western capes of Bristol Bay for several hours on January 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cape Newenham ASOS station (PAEH) observed 0 to quarter mile visibilities with winds gusting to over 55 mph from 9:00 AM AKST January 30th to 1:00 AM AKST January 31st. Webcams in Goodnews Bay revealed near whiteout conditions at 10:30 AM on January 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center near the Alaska Peninsula combined with building high pressure over the Arctic Ocean to produce strong winds from the northeast. At times blizzard and near blizzard conditions resulted as the winds pick up loose snow from the surface of the sea ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north/northeast winds originated over the southeastern Chukchi Sea and continued southwards through the Bering strait.  The strong winds impacted the northern coast of St Lawrence Island throughout the day with winds consistently gusting over 60 mph throughout the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center near the Alaska Peninsula combined with building high pressure over the Arctic Ocean to produce strong winds from the northeast. At times blizzard and near blizzard conditions resulted as the winds pick up loose snow from the surface of the sea ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds blowing along the northwest arctic coastline produced very strong winds with gusts as high as 62 mph at Point Hope.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure center in the southern Bering Sea moved north into the Bering Strait and brought with it heavy Snowfall for Alaska's west coast along with very strong easterly surface winds. The result was a prolonged period of snow and very low visibilities due to blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from the Kotzebue ASOS and local NWS employee revealed heavy snow and high wind setting in early on the morning of the 17th. By afternoon blizzard conditions with wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph and visibilities around 1/4 mile prevailed for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure center in the southern Bering Sea moved north into the Bering Strait and brought with it heavy Snowfall for Alaska's west coast along with very strong easterly surface winds. The result was a prolonged period of snow and very low visibilities due to blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snowfall combined to bring Blizzard conditions to much of Saint Lawrence Island through the morning hours. Wind gusts of 35 mph or greater were reported by the GBXA2 AWOS while visual confirmation of extremely low visibilities were observed on the FAA webcam at Gambell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold upper level low tracked from the Bering Coastline of Western Alaska into the interior towards the end of the month. This resulted in periods of locally heavy snow and strong winds which brought low visibilities due to blowing snow. In addition, the coldest air of the season arrived across northern Alaska in the wake of this storm as a large Arctic high settled in behind the storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska DOT reported Blizzard conditions through Windy Pass along the Parks Highway. DOT crews reported not being able to tell when the snow stopped due to the amount of blowing snow and poor visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure set up off the coast of Yakutat causing bands of snow showers to train over the region. This made for a long duration event with some locations seeing equally long breaks between the snow showers. Warning level rates were observed during the bands of snow and observers added them together.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports through the morning of the 30th showed heavy snow on Douglas Island that had accumulated to 6 inches overnight, some of that likely included lighter snow that had fallen during the day before. Heavy snow continued over this portion of the Juneau Borough through the 30th with a measurement of 15 inches at lower elevations of West Juneau by 304pm. The school district use the snow bus routes for Douglas and cancelled afternoon activities. Other parts of Juneau saw much less, totaling 5 to 9 inches of snow in the Valley and out the road between the 28th and 31st of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure set up off the coast of Yakutat causing bands of snow showers to train over the region. This made for a long duration event with some locations seeing equally long breaks between the snow showers. Warning level rates were observed during the bands of snow and observers added them together.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hoonah COOP measured 3.6 inches of snow at 730am on the 30th. The airport AWOS was not reporting during this time, making start and end times estimated, but surrounding areas reported snowfall rates increase after the morning COOP report and ended in the evening. Therefore the COOP measurement of 9.1 inches at 8am on the 31st fell in approximately 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure set up off the coast of Yakutat causing bands of snow showers to train over the region. This made for a long duration event with some locations seeing equally long breaks between the snow showers. Warning level rates were observed during the bands of snow and observers added them together.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee in Yakutat measured 8.4 inches of snow the morning of the 29th for a daily CoCoRaHS report, but noted that most of that had fallen by late evening on the 28th. More rounds of heavy snow fell on the 29th with an additional 7.8 inches measured by the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal band advancing north overran cold air previously put in place by an outflow event. Although areas further south saw a fairly quick changeover from snow to rain, Haines, Skagway, and their respective highways maintained snow for a far longer duration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Klondike Highway was closed due to an avalanche, with a report received in the afternoon that the road had been re-opened on January 5th. Strong northerly winds with gusts of 45 to 55 mph and falling snow were occurring prior to the avalanche. DOT reported shortly after 9 AM the next morning January 6th that a minimum of 5 of new snow had fallen along parts of the Klondike Highway, with corresponding difficult road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal band advancing north overran cold air previously put in place by an outflow event. Although areas further south saw a fairly quick changeover from snow to rain, Haines, Skagway, and their respective highways maintained snow for a far longer duration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter measured 6 inches of snow had fallen throughout the day Sunday the 5th in Haines with light snow adding another 3 inches by the following morning. Another trained spotter south of Haines on the Chilkat Peninsula reported 9 inches of new snow at 1140pm on the 5th, with heavy snowfall still underway. Five inches of that had been since around 4 pm Sunday. Snow ended early on the 6th with a short period of freezing rain reported at the airport. The COOP observer in town had 9.7 inches of snow during this period. Measurements further to the north up the highway were lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shift in the steering pattern opened the path for a low to track across the southern panhandle, bringing with it high winds. Just north of the high winds, increased precipitation rates caused a change to heavy snow for the Kake area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at Salmon Landing in Ketchikan reported a maximum wind gust of 63 mph at 1232pm on the 15th. In addition, the State Ferry Columbia was unable to dock in Ketchikan for a period of time, and the scheduled flight from Sitka to Ketchikan was unable to land, both due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow showers tracked over the southern panhandle early Friday morning with quickly accumulating snow. The snow showers fell in two bands, one across the southern panhandle and another across Sitka to Juneau on Friday. The northern band very slowly track northward to Yakutat late Friday night into early Saturday morning causing an advisory for Yakutat to be upgraded to a warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell overnight, but quickly became moderate to heavy with several inches stacking up between 6 to 9am. Greatest amounts were reported with social media during the 8am hour, including 6 to 8 inches on the south side of Ketchikan. The band of snow showers shifted north of the area by noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving into the northern panhandle brought heavy snow to the Klondike Highway. The snow was primarily located at higher elevations with rain reported at sea level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 8 inches of snow fell on the Klondike Highway overnight from mile 12 to the border. Southerly winds were gusting 20 to 30 mph during this time at the Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system sweeping into SE AK brought high winds to Yakutat as a front raced northward, and strong winds across much of the northern panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yakutat ASOS station recorded wind gusts in excess of 60 mph for a 4 hour period during a frontal passage. This included a peak gust of 66 mph at 450pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shift in the steering pattern opened the path for a low to track across the southern panhandle, bringing with it high winds. Just north of the high winds, increased precipitation rates caused a change to heavy snow for the Kake area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations across Annette Island measured high winds for approximately two hours on the 15th. The Packing Company measured peak gusts of 62 mph at 1035pm and the Yellow Hill Station, at 479 ft elevation, reported a peak gust of 83 mph at 1230pm.

CZ_NAME: WESTERN KUPREANOF ISLAND AND KUIU ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shift in the steering pattern opened the path for a low to track across the southern panhandle, bringing with it high winds. Just north of the high winds, increased precipitation rates caused a change to heavy snow for the Kake area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Kake reported that rain changed to snow at about 940am on the 15th with visibility falling to a half mile. The spotter measured 4.5 inches by 249pm with heavy rates continuing. Another spotter SW of Kake in Security Bay reported similar conditions with a measurement of 8 inches and reported it had changed back to rain at 335pm. The change over to rain in Kake happened about an hour later with precipitation ending shortly after.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold wind chills due to northerly outflow winds and low temperatures along the Klondike Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Klondike Highway RWIS station at White Pass measured strong northerly winds with gusts of 35 to 45 mph and cold temperatures, causing wind chills to fall below -40 degrees for more than 2 days. The coldest wind chills were -43F at 940am on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building north into Alaska created a strong gradient across northwestern Alaska, resulting in a fetch of strong east winds along the North Slope where blowing snow created blizzard conditions while storm fore winds blew parallel to the Northwest Arctic and Bering coastlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds consistently gusted at or above 60 mph for approximately 18 hours with a peak gust of 77 mph registered at Cape Lisburne LRRS (PALU).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building north into Alaska created a strong gradient across northwestern Alaska, resulting in a fetch of strong east winds along the North Slope where blowing snow created blizzard conditions while storm fore winds blew parallel to the Northwest Arctic and Bering coastlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds consistently gusted at or above 50 mph for approximately 7 hours with a peak gust of 64 mph registered at Wales (PAIW).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building north into Alaska created a strong gradient across northwestern Alaska, resulting in a fetch of strong east winds along the North Slope where blowing snow created blizzard conditions while storm fore winds blew parallel to the Northwest Arctic and Bering coastlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions prevailed for about 9 hours at Gambell where the local AWOS (PAGM) recorded 1/4 mile visibility and wind gusts between 45 mph and 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building north into Alaska created a strong gradient across northwestern Alaska, resulting in a fetch of strong east winds along the North Slope where blowing snow created blizzard conditions while storm fore winds blew parallel to the Northwest Arctic and Bering coastlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions began in the late evening and continued well into the night at Nuiqsut (PAQT). Observations consistently showed 40 mph winds gusts and visibility at around 1/4 mile. ASOS observations were lost overnight, but webcams provided visual reference that the blizzard conditions continued well into the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building north into Alaska created a strong gradient across northwestern Alaska, resulting in a fetch of strong east winds along the North Slope where blowing snow created blizzard conditions while storm fore winds blew parallel to the Northwest Arctic and Bering coastlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barter Island AWOS (PABR) reported near constant wind gusts of 45 mph to 50mph and visibilities fluctuating between 1/10th and 1/4th mild through the night as Blizzard conditions persisted. Heavy snow as also falling at the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building north into Alaska created a strong gradient across northwestern Alaska, resulting in a fetch of strong east winds along the North Slope where blowing snow created blizzard conditions while storm fore winds blew parallel to the Northwest Arctic and Bering coastlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barter Island experienced a short lived blizzard while heavy snow continued to fall. Winds gusted to around 40 mph for approximately 3 hours while the visibility dropped to 1/4 mile at Barter Island (PABA).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high end gale to storm force system moved in from the south, bringing damaging winds in the morning of the 26th to the southern panhandle while heavy precipitation rates brought snow to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska Marine Highway Ferry Columbia could not dock as scheduled on the morning of the 26th due to winds and seas. The Inter Island Ferry was cancelled for the day in anticipation of the storm. There were several flight cancellations in and out of Ketchikan due to the wind. A ship report in a cove on the north side of Gravina Island estimated 60mph winds at 1250pm. Peak winds at other mesonet stations in the area ranged from 46 to 53 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure set up off the coast of Yakutat causing bands of snow showers to train over the region. This made for a long duration event with some locations seeing equally long breaks between the snow showers. Warning level rates were observed during the bands of snow and observers added them together.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pelican COOP measured 5.3 inches of snow at their morning measurement on the 30th, with snowfall rates likely picking up after. Start and end times are estimated based on surrounding areas and snow ending in the evening. Therefore, with the COOP measurement of 2.7 inches the following morning, 8 inches of snow fell in approximately 19 hours. Overall totals were greater than this due to snow that had occurred the day before.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air moved out of Canada and interior Alaska and outflow winds brought wind chills down to warning criteria levels at White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Klondike Highway RWIS at White Pass recorded temperatures in the teens below zero with sustained wind speeds of more that 20mph during the early morning hours of Saturday February 1st. This resulted in wind chills as cold as 41 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high end gale to storm force system moved in from the south, bringing damaging winds in the morning of the 26th to the southern panhandle while heavy precipitation rates brought snow to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hydaburg Seaplane Base AWOS started measuring gusts above 60mph at 420am on the 26th and continued through mid-day, including a peak gust of 81mph at 509am. A social media report shared a photo of damage to a porch roof from this event. Other peak winds across Prince of Wales Island ranged from a 57mph gust in Craig to sustained winds of 54mph at the Kasaan FAA webcam. The Inter Island Ferry was cancelled for the day in anticipation of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high end gale to storm force system moved in from the south, bringing damaging winds in the morning of the 26th to the southern panhandle while heavy precipitation rates brought snow to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station located at the Annette Island Packing Company, located along the shoreline of Metlakatla, started to measure wind gusts in excess of 60mph at 140am on the 26th and continued through that afternoon, including a peak gust of 80mph at 425am. Other high wind gusts were measured at the elevation sites of Yellow Hill and Melson Lake. A NWS Employee reported that two power poles were leaning over or snapped on Walden Point Rd, closing the road and nearby stores. A later report from the local tribal government indicated damage had occurred to the historic Boat House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure set up off the coast of Yakutat causing bands of snow showers to train over the region. This made for a long duration event with some locations seeing equally long breaks between the snow showers. Warning level rates were observed during the bands of snow and observers added them together.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observer at the Petersburg Airport had 5 inches of snow by 1pm on the 30th and noted 2 inches from the day before. Periods of heavy snow continued through the evening of the 30th, but was swept away by the wind, making it difficult to measure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high end gale to storm force system moved in from the south, bringing damaging winds in the morning of the 26th to the southern panhandle while heavy precipitation rates brought snow to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the Chilkat Valley around dawn on the 26th as rates picked up. A trained spotter at 26 mile reported 7 inches had fallen by 1pm. Haines Customs similarly measured 8 inches at 147pm that day and a total of 14 inches by their following morning COOP report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure set up off the coast of Yakutat causing bands of snow showers to train over the region. This made for a long duration event with some locations seeing equally long breaks between the snow showers. Warning level rates were observed during the bands of snow and observers added them together.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bands of snow moved through on the 29th and DOT in Haines estimated 8 to 12 inches of snow had fallen by early morning on the 30th. Other observers in town confirmed these amounts in the 8am hour, however the Customs station 40 miles to the NW only measured 3.5 inches. One spotter in town noted that around 10 inches of the 12 inches that he measured had fallen with the second band overnight. Periods of light snow continued through the morning on the 30th, then strong W-NW winds increased with gusts of 30-40 mph and blowing snow causing visibility down to 1 mile into the evening. The snowfall through this later period did not accumulate as much.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure set up off the coast of Yakutat causing bands of snow showers to train over the region. This made for a long duration event with some locations seeing equally long breaks between the snow showers. Warning level rates were observed during the bands of snow and observers added them together.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the early morning hours of the 30th, with periods of heavy rates, resulting in a social media report of 6 inches by 1235pm in Craig on the west side of Prince of Wales Island. Another report from a traveler from Hollis had only 4 inches there on the east side of the island, but estimated nearly 16 inches over the Hollis to Klawock Highway at 152pm. Periods of heavy snow continued through the late hours before ending after just over 24 hours. The COOP in Klawock also noted significant snowfall during this event, with a depth of 18 inches by the end of it. Another social media report from Whale Pass on the NE side of the island estimated 12 inches of snow from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In early May a weather pattern developed that placed a strong high over the Arctic Ocean and a strong low in the northern Gulf of Alaska. This resulted in rather windy conditions across much of northern Alaska. Due to the time of year, there was not a lot of blowable snow on the ground along the coastline, but further away from the coast and along the Dalton Highway there was enough snow for the strong winds to produce blowing snow and very poor visibilities for a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PASC (Deadhorse) did not reach Blizzard Criteria and webcams showed much of the visibility reduction was due to fog and less so to blowing snow. However 511 reports (Alaska DOT) and blowing snow satellite products indicated blizzard conditions. 511 Report had peak wind of 52 mph, large snow drift near MM392 and visibility down to 1 delineator, approximately 500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance aloft crossed Alaska's Arctic coastline resulting in a widespread area of snow with totals of 1 to 3 inches along it's path. Strong westerly winds also developed at the same time with the strongest gradient producing much stronger winds along the northeast Arctic coastline. Where the winds and falling snow overlapped, blizzard conditions developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barter Island AWOS (PABA) reported Blizzard conditions with wind gusts as high as 40 mph and several hours of visibilities at or below 1/4 mile in blowing snow. While the event persisted for just over 24 hours, there were a few observations of up to 1/2 mile visibility at times during the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In early May a weather pattern developed that placed a strong high over the Arctic Ocean and a strong low in the northern Gulf of Alaska. This resulted in rather windy conditions across much of northern Alaska. Due to the time of year, there was not a lot of blowable snow on the ground along the coastline, but further away from the coast and along the Dalton Highway there was enough snow for the strong winds to produce blowing snow and very poor visibilities for a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early morning 511 reports (Alaska DOT) and blowing snow satellite products indicated blizzard conditions. 511 Report had peak wind of 52 mph, large snow drift near MM392 and visibility down to 1 delineator, approximately 500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On late March 8th, an occuded front from a Kamchatka low in the Bering reached Nunivak Island and the Kuskokwim Delta Coast. The front, combined with a cold air mass was able to bring strong gale force winds to the region. Surface Winds were easterly and therefore, offshore. Winds gusted to over 40 mph at times. The front occluded before it reached the Pribilof Islands, limiting warm air advection. This, combined with mostly ice-covered waters around Nunivak Island, allowed temperatures to stay colder than otherwise. As a result, Nunivak Island received blizzard conditions for many hours, extending into the early morning hours of March 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk ASOS (PAMY) reported heavy snowfall, winds gusting to over 40 mph, and visibility below one quarter of a mile from 8:55 PM AKST March 8th to 1:45 AM AKST March 9th. Webcams overlooking the village comfirmed this heavy snowfall and low visiblity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ice breakup along the Kuskokwim River during the spring of 2025 produced less ice jam flooding compared to an average spring season.  Still, areas of mostly minor flooding did occur with occasional ice jams along parts of the river channel as the breakup front moved towards the Kuskokwim Delta from late April into early May.  One of the more notable ice jams formed on May 5th just downstream from Upper Kalskag and Lower Kalskag, resulting in a brief spike in water levels and flooding of local roadways near both communities before the downstream ice jam broke on May 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Staff from the Anchorage River Forecast Center spotted an ice jam downstream from Kalskag during their aerial river watch survey on the afternoon of May 5th.  The ice jam caused water to rapidly rise and flood the road connecting the Upper Kalskag and Lower Kalskag communities.  School children were taken back and forth by boat to travel between the two villages while flood waters were at their highest.  By May 7th, the downstream ice jam broke up and allowed water levels to recede to normal levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable setup for strong thunderstorms over parts of the Alaska Interior and down across the Middle Kuskokwim Valley took shape during the afternoon and evening of 6/17.  Very warm temperatures in the 70s and 80s, dew points in the 50s, and a shortwave trough moving near the Alaska Range from Southcentral Alaska contributed to moderate instability and an overall environment conducive to intense thunderstorms.  Thunderstorms with frequent lighting first developed over the western Interior on the afternoon of 6/17, then moved south due to northerly steering flow into parts of the Kuskokwim Valley during the afternoon and evening hours late in the day.  The GLD lightning feed detected extremely frequent lighting with these thunderstorms, supporting convective-driven red flag conditions across much of the Middle Kuskokwim Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Source is the global lightning detection network (GLD) and radar/satellite observations.  Strong thunderstorms produced frequent lightning as they moved near the western Alaska Range during the evening and overnight on 6/17, including near Lime Village.  The frequent lighting led to significant fire weather concerns (red flag conditions due to lightning) during the thunderstorm event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system approaching a strong ridge of high pressure in place for several days across Southcentral Alaska resulted in a favorable setup for significant fire weather concerns across portions of the Copper Basin on the afternoon of 6/21.  Gusty south winds enhanced by terrain developed out of the Copper River Valley as temperatures climbed into the low 80s and as relative humidities dropped to near 20% for several hours, leading to red flag conditions and increased fire activity noted on satellite near two existing wildfires over parts of the northern Copper Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gulkana ASOS recorded red flag conditions (RH less than 25%, 15 mph winds gusting to 25 mph, and temperatures of 75 to 81 degrees) from around 1 PM AKST to 4 PM AKST on the afternoon of 6/21.  Two ongoing wildfires farther to the north showed increased activity and smoke on satellite products around the same timeframe as winds reached maximum intensity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low from the North Pacific moved into Bristol Bay sending a warm front north through the Interior where strong Chinook winds developed through the Alaska Range gaps and up into the central interior of Alaska. Meanwhile strong high pressure over the Beaufort Sea helped produce strong east winds along the Arctic coastline of the North Slope resulting in blizzard conditions across much of the arctic coastline of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in the mid morning hours and eventually began gusting to between 35 and 45 mph creating blizzard conditions for over 12 hours at Point Lay (PPIZ).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large north Pacific low moved south of the Eastern Aleutians/Alaska Peninsula Regions on April 3rd. This, combined with a ridge to the west over Shemya, an upper level low in the Central Aleutians, as well as cold air advection from the north allowed for tight pressure gradients. The tighter gradients led to strong, gusty winds that reached storm force strength from the Eastern Aleutians to the Kuskokwim Delta. Heavy snowfall also accompanied this low that, combined with the gusty winds, brought about blizzard conditions in the aforementioned regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A representative of the Unalaska Department of Public Safety reported heavy snowfall and gusty winds at 11:30 AKST on April 3rd. She reported dangerous and slick road conditions. The Emergency Manager of Unalaska followed up at 4:00 PM AKDT the same day that road conditions remained extremely difficult due to dangerous road conditions. Two hours later, the Unalaska Department of Public Safety issued a Category 2 Travel Advisory advising caution when traveling due to high winds and blowing snow causing whiteout conditions. Furthermore, the Dutch Harbor ASOS weather station reported low visibility between one mile and half a mile from 8:30 AM AKDT to 10:00 PM AKDT April 3rd. Webcams in the Unalaska area confirmed this low visibilty. Winds were gusting to 40 to 46 kts at 7:00 PM AKDT April 3rd. On April 4th, The Emergency Manager of Unalaska reported 3 to 4 ft drifts over roads and parking lots with one related car accident. A second Emergency Manager called from their house at 500 ft and reported sustained wind speeds of 45 mph gusting to 65 mph in the morning hours. He also reported snow drifts as large as 12 to 15 ft tall and 8 to 10 inches of snow accumulation. In Akutan, visibility below half a mile was shown by a webcam in the evening hours of April 3rd. A TV meteorologist from KTUU relayed that a source in Akutan reported 8 to 10 inches of snow accumulation in the town with 2 to 4 ft snow berms on April 4th. Winds gusted to 60 mph.

CZ_NAME: KUSKOKWIM DELTA COAST AND NUNIVAK ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large north Pacific low moved south of the Eastern Aleutians/Alaska Peninsula Regions on April 3rd. This, combined with a ridge to the west over Shemya, an upper level low in the Central Aleutians, as well as cold air advection from the north allowed for tight pressure gradients. The tighter gradients led to strong, gusty winds that reached storm force strength from the Eastern Aleutians to the Kuskokwim Delta. Heavy snowfall also accompanied this low that, combined with the gusty winds, brought about blizzard conditions in the aforementioned regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toksook Bay and Chefornak webcams displayed visibility lower than half a mile at 7:30 PM AKDT on April 3rd. Conditions further deteriorated with winds becoming sustained at 30 to 40 mph by 11:00 PM AKDT the same day. Blizzard conditions persisted in these settlements until the evening hours of April 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A duo of low pressure systems were meandering around one another in the Eastern Bering and Southwestern Alaska mainland regions on April 11th. Associated frontal systems drove strong winds and heavy precipitation to the Eastern Aleutians, Kuskokwim Delta coast, Western Capes of Bristol Bay, and the Alaska Peninsula. Winds remained northerly in the Bering, allowing precipitation to remain as snow due to cold air advection. Blowing snow resulted from the strong winds and heavy snowfall in the Alaska Peninsula. This reduced visibilities for several hours in Cold Bay and False Pass. The lows merged as they tracked east, setting up a deformation band in the Alaska Peninsula. As a result, improvements in weather were delayed until the night of April 13th. As the low pushed into the Gulf of Alaska, winds channeled through the Turnagain Arm, driving gusty winds into the Anchorage Hillside on April 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The False Pass ASOS (PAKF) reported heavy snow, winds gusting to over 40 mph, and visibilities less than a quarter of a mile starting at 7:36 AM AKDT on April 13th and continuing until 11:15 AM AKDT the same day. The Cold Bay ASOS (PACD) reported similar conditions from 9:20 AM AKDT April 13th until 11:55 AM AKDT the same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved up from the North Pacific into Southcentral Alaska and produced widespread moderate rainfall between August 27th and August 29th.  Strong southerly flow during the rainfall event caused south-facing slopes of the mountains across Southcentral to get the highest rainfall totals due to enhancement from upslope flow, including the north end of the Susitna Valley, where 2.5 to locally 6 inches of rainfall was recorded between 8/27 and 8/29.  The runoff from the heavy rainfall pushed multiple waterways across the Susitna Valley into moderate flood stage from around 8/29 to 8/31, including sections of the Talkeetna River, Yentna River and Moose Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yentna River at Lake Creek entered flood stage on the afternoon of August 28th, then rose to a record crest around 22.56 feet in moderate flood stage by August 29th.  River levels remained near the record crest through the morning of August 31st, then began dropping back below flood stage by the evening of August 31st.  Observers along the river between Skwentna and Lake Creek reported that numerous cabins in the area were flooded during this time, with many cabins also left without power due to their generators flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved up from the North Pacific into Southcentral Alaska and produced widespread moderate rainfall between August 27th and August 29th.  Strong southerly flow during the rainfall event caused south-facing slopes of the mountains across Southcentral to get the highest rainfall totals due to enhancement from upslope flow, including the north end of the Susitna Valley, where 2.5 to locally 6 inches of rainfall was recorded between 8/27 and 8/29.  The runoff from the heavy rainfall pushed multiple waterways across the Susitna Valley into moderate flood stage from around 8/29 to 8/31, including sections of the Talkeetna River, Yentna River and Moose Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flood gauge near Talkeetna at the confluence of the Talkeetna River and Susitna River rose into flood stage around 12 PM AKST August 29th, crested just below major flood stage at 14.93 ft around 11 PM AKST August 29th, then receded back to below minor flood stage by 12 PM AKST August 30th.  This was the third highest crest on record for the gauge, and highest water level recorded since 2012. Pictures from Talkeetna by an NWS employee on 8/30 showed overbank flooding at the end of Main Street.  Low lying areas of Talkeetna, particularly the lower west side of town, experienced flood waters from high water on the Susitna. Worst impacts were to the Alaska Dream Cabins that are located at the west end of Main Street / B Street and by the water. The Alaska River Forecast Center found high water marks of 1-2 ft on several buildings. Despite some raising of buildings, Water reached the lowest few inches to foot of many of the buildings. The Talkeetna River Park and campground experienced flooding as well as the low-lying areas between the campground and revetment.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station (FW8205) in Potter Valley winds gusting over 65 mph from 1:21 AM to 3:10 AM AKDT on April 14th. Wind gusts peaked at 83 mph from 1:36 to 1:41 AM AKDT the same day. Other weather stations in the Anchorage Hillside reported winds gusting to over 70 mph for the same time period. An NWS employee observed a 12 - 15 inch diameter conifer down in Potter Valley alongside Bluebell Dr after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed and moved north into the southern Alaska Peninsula between April 15th and April 16th. A band of heavy snow and strong northerly winds developed and moved across Akutan and Unalaska. The settlements saw impacts including poor visibility, bad road conditions, and large snow drifts. Higher elevations saw more snowfall and larger snowdrifts. As the low pushed eastward, an associated strong front moved into Southcentral Alaska on April 16th. The front caused strong winds to develop through mountain gaps and higher elevations of the Anchorage Hillside and along the Turnagain Arm, especially in Portage Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Unalaska ASOS (PADU) reported persistent heavy snowfall, winds gusting to over 60 mph, and low visibility from 5:00 AM AKDT April 15th to 11:00 PM AKDT the same day. The Emergency Manager of Unalaska reported that there was heavy snow from sea level to higher elevations. They estimated around 12 inches of accumulation across the island of Unalaska with significant drifting at upper elevations of the city. The Emergency Manager office issued a level 2 travel advisory due to poor road conditions. The weather was a factor in the closing of several businesses from 4/15 - 4/16. One car slid off the road due to the difficult travel conditions brought about by snow covered roads and poor visibility. In Akutan, a news reporter relayed that 10 to 15 inches of snow fell in the settlement with winds gusting to 60 to 80 mph. Snow berms/drifts were built up to 4 feet.

A band of heavy snow and strong northerly winds developed and moved across Akutan and Unalaska. The settlements saw impacts including poor visibility, bad road conditions, and large snow drifts. Higher elevations saw more snowfall and larger snowdrifts. As the low pushed eastward, an associated strong front moved into Southcentral Alaska on April 16th. The front caused strong winds to develop through mountain gaps and higher elevations of the Anchorage Hillside and along the Turnagain Arm, especially in Portage Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station in Bear Valley (G5830) reported gusty winds in excess of 70 mph beginning April 16th at 8:16 AM AKDT. Wind gusts peaked at 10:31 AM AKDT the same day, gusting to 91 mph until 10:36 AM AKDT. Gusty winds commonly surpassing 80 mph remained until 1:52 PM that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed and moved north into the southern Alaska Peninsula between April 15th and April 16th.
